8 categories

claims.claimText: The Kenton R. Miller Award is constituted by the World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA), which is one of the six technical commissions of the IUCN.

claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the parent organization of the award.

claims.claimText: The Kenton R. Miller Award is presented every two years to honor individuals demonstrating innovation and leadership in conserving national parks and protected areas.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Honors individuals who show innovation and leadership in conserving national parks and protected areas. Presented every two years by the IUCN-WCPA.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the frequency and specific target of the award.
distractorHooks: Presented annually instead of biennially

claims.claimText: The Director of Kaziranga National Park is the first Indian to receive the IUCN's Kenton R. Miller Award.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Kaziranga Director becomes first Indian to receive IUCN’s Kenton R. Miller Award for innovation in national parks.
claims.examUse: Fact-based MCQ or correct-pair question regarding recent environmental award recipients.
distractorHooks: Director of Jim Corbett National Park
First Asian recipient instead of first Indian

claims.claimText: The IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC) was established in 1949 and operates as a science-based network of volunteer experts.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Established in 1949, it is a science-based network of more than 10,500 volunteer experts.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the origin and composition of the IUCN SSC.
distractorHooks: Established during the 1992 Earth Summit

claims.claimText: Indian wildlife conservationist Vivek Menon was elected as the Chair of the IUCN Species Survival Commission for the 2025-2029 term.

claims.examUse: Current affairs statement in a multi-statement question regarding Indian representation in global environmental bodies.
distractorHooks: Elected as the head of UNEP

claims.claimText: The role of the IUCN Species Survival Commission is to provide information to the IUCN regarding biodiversity conservation, species value, and ecosystem health.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Role: To provide information to IUCN on biodiversity conservation, the inherent value of species, their role in ecosystem health and functioning, etc.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing the specific mandate and function of the IUCN SSC.
distractorHooks: Enforces international wildlife trade laws

claims.claimText: The IUCN World Heritage Outlook 4 Report categorizes the Western Ghats, Manas National Park, and Sundarbans National Park as being of 'significant concern'.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: It categorises Western Ghats and 2 national parks (NP) in India – Assam’s Manas NP and West Bengal’s Sundarbans NP as being of “significant concern”.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the conservation status of Indian sites in the IUCN World Heritage Outlook Report.
distractorHooks: Swapping the names of the national parks (e.g., Kaziranga instead of Manas)
Changing the status category to 'Critical' or 'Good'

claims.claimText: Khangchendzonga National Park is the only Indian site that remained 'Good' in the Conservation Outlook Rating of the IUCN World Heritage Outlook 4 Report.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Only Khangchendzonga National Park remained ‘Good’ in Conservation Outlook Rating.
claims.examUse: Fact-based statement identifying the only 'Good' rated Indian site in the report.
distractorHooks: Naming another prominent national park (e.g., Jim Corbett, Keoladeo) as the only 'Good' site
Stating that no Indian site achieved a 'Good' rating

claims.claimText: The conservation outlook status of Sundarbans National Park deteriorated from 'Good with some Concerns' to 'Significant Concerns' between 2020 and 2025.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Sundarbans National Park: Witnessed Deterioration in its conservation outlook status from ‘Good with some Concerns’ to ‘Significant Concerns’ from 2020-2025.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question tracking the changing conservation status of major Indian ecological sites.
distractorHooks: Stating the status improved rather than deteriorated
Attributing this specific deterioration to a different national park like Manas

claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the conservation status of the Asiatic Lion.

claims.sectionTitle: Green Status Assessment for the Lion

distractorHooks: Asiatic lions have thicker and longer manes than African lions

claims.sectionTitle: UNESCO's World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) & Cold Desert BR
claims.claimText: The World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) is managed by UNESCO's Man and the Biosphere (MAB) Programme, which was launched in 1971.

claims.sectionTitle: UNESCO's World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) & Cold Desert BR

distractorHooks: Designations made by the UN General Assembly

claims.sectionTitle: UNESCO's World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) & Cold Desert BR
claims.claimText: The Cold Desert Biosphere Reserve in Himachal Pradesh encompasses Pin Valley National Park, Chandratal, and the Sarchu and Kibber Wildlife Sanctuaries.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Cold Desert BR (Himachal Pradesh) covers Pin Valley NP, Chandratal, and Sarchu & Kibber WLS.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question mapping protected areas to their respective Biosphere Reserves.
distractorHooks: Located in Ladakh or Uttarakhand instead of Himachal Pradesh

claims.sectionTitle: UNESCO's World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) & Cold Desert BR
claims.claimText: The Cold Desert Biosphere Reserve is located in a rain-shadow zone and is characterized by a distinct Buddhist culture and fauna such as the Snow Leopard and Himalayan Ibex.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Cold Desert BR lies in a rain-shadow zone with distinct Buddhist culture (Gompas, Chorten) and fauna like Snow Leopard and Himalayan Ibex.
claims.examUse: Feature-based identification question describing a specific Biosphere Reserve.
distractorHooks: Located in a high-rainfall zone

claims.claimText: The Wildlife Protection Act (WPA) 2022 Amendment reduced the number of schedules to four, designating Schedule III for protected plants and Schedule IV for CITES species.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: WPA 2022 Amendment reduced schedules to 4: Sch I (highest protection), Sch II (lesser protection), Sch III (plants), Sch IV (CITES species).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the features of the WPA 2022 Amendment.
distractorHooks: Claiming the amendment retained 6 schedules
Swapping Schedule III (plants) with Schedule IV (CITES)

claims.claimText: The National Board for Wildlife, established under the Wildlife Protection Act, is chaired by the Prime Minister.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Establishes the National Board for Wildlife, chaired by the Prime Minister.
claims.examUse: Institution-feature question identifying the chairperson of statutory environmental bodies.
distractorHooks: Chaired by the Minister of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

claims.claimText: The Chief Wild Life Warden is authorized to permit the hunting of Schedule I and II animals if they pose a threat to human life or are diseased beyond recovery.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Chief Wild Life Warden can permit hunting of Schedule I and II animals under Sections 11 and 12 if they are dangerous to human life or diseased beyond recovery.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on exceptions to the prohibition of hunting protected species.
distractorHooks: Only the Central Government can permit hunting of Schedule I animals

claims.claimText: Prior permission from the Central Government is mandatory for the translocation of Schedule I animals.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Translocation of Schedule I animals requires prior permission from the Central Government.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the regulatory approvals required for wildlife management.
distractorHooks: Translocation requires permission only from the State Government

claims.sectionTitle: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
claims.claimText: The National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) issued the Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) has issued the new Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the regulatory body responsible for issuing the Access and Benefit Sharing Regulation 2025.
distractorHooks: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)
State Biodiversity Boards (SBBs)

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
claims.claimText: The Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025 was notified by the National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) to replace the 2014 Guidelines.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Notified by the National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) to replace 2014 Guidelines.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the notifying authority for ABS regulations.
distractorHooks: Notified directly by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
claims.claimText: The 2025 ABS Regulations explicitly include Digital Sequence Information (DSI), which are digital representations of genetic resources like DNA/RNA, as a mode of benefit sharing.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Includes Digital Sequence Information (DSI) - digital representations of genetic resources like DNA/RNA.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the scope of biological resources covered under the new ABS regulations.
distractorHooks: Excludes Digital Sequence Information (DSI)
Applies only to physical biological samples

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
claims.claimText: Access to cultivated medicinal plants notified under the Biological Diversity Act 2002 is exempted from the 2025 ABS Regulations.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Exempts access to cultivated medicinal plants notified under BDA 2002.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing knowledge of exemptions under the ABS framework.
distractorHooks: All medicinal plants, including wild ones, are exempted
Cultivated medicinal plants are subject to the highest benefit-sharing slab

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
claims.claimText: Under the 2025 ABS Regulations, entities with an annual turnover of up to 5 Crore are subject to a 'nil' benefit sharing slab.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Benefit sharing slabs range from nil (up to 5 Cr turnover) to 0.6% (above 250 Cr turnover).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the financial thresholds and exemptions for benefit sharing.
distractorHooks: A flat benefit sharing rate applies to all entities regardless of turnover
Entities with turnover up to 50 Crore are exempted

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
claims.claimText: High-Value Biological Resources, such as red sanders, require a minimum of 5% of auction or sale proceeds to be shared as benefits under the 2025 ABS Regulations.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: High-Value Biological Resources (e.g., red sanders) require minimum 5% of auction/sale proceeds.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on specific provisions for high-value biological resources.
distractorHooks: Maximum of 5% of auction proceeds
Minimum of 10% of auction proceeds
Exempted from benefit sharing if auctioned by the government

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
claims.claimText: The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) was adopted at the 1992 Rio Earth Summit and India is a member state.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: CBD adopted at 1992 Rio Earth Summit; India is a member.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the origin and membership of the CBD.
distractorHooks: Claiming the CBD was adopted at a different summit (e.g., Stockholm 1972 or Johannesburg 2002)
Stating India is not a member or has not ratified the CBD

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
claims.claimText: The three main goals of the Convention on Biological Diversity are conservation, sustainable use, and fair and equitable benefit-sharing.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Three main goals of CBD: Conservation, Sustainable use, Fair and Equitable Benefit-Sharing.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the core objectives of the CBD.
distractorHooks: Adding a fabricated fourth goal like 'eradicating invasive species globally'
Confusing the goals with those of UNFCCC or UNCCD

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
claims.claimText: The Cartagena Protocol, adopted under the CBD, deals specifically with Biosafety.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Cartagena Protocol (2003) deals with Biosafety.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching environmental protocols to their subject matter.
distractorHooks: Swapping the subject matter of Cartagena (Biosafety) with Nagoya (ABS)
Claiming Cartagena deals with ozone depletion or hazardous waste

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
claims.claimText: The Nagoya Protocol is a legally-binding agreement for the fair and equitable sharing of benefits arising from genetic resources.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Nagoya Protocol (2010) is a legally-binding agreement for fair and equitable sharing of benefits from genetic resources.
claims.examUse: Comparison question distinguishing between the legal nature of Nagoya Protocol and Bonn Guidelines.
distractorHooks: Stating the Nagoya Protocol is voluntary
Swapping Nagoya Protocol's focus (ABS) with Cartagena's focus (Biosafety)

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
claims.claimText: The Bonn Guidelines are voluntary guidelines for Access and Benefit-Sharing (ABS).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Bonn Guidelines (2002) are voluntary guidelines for ABS.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the nature of international ABS frameworks.
distractorHooks: Claiming the Bonn Guidelines are legally binding
Confusing the Bonn Guidelines with the Bonn Convention (CMS)

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
claims.claimText: A Multilateral Mechanism for the use of Digital Sequence Information (DSI) was adopted at CBD COP16 in 2024.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Multilateral Mechanism for DSI use adopted at CBD COP16 (2024).
claims.examUse: Current affairs statement-based question on recent developments in CBD COPs.
distractorHooks: Stating the DSI mechanism was adopted at UNFCCC COP28
Claiming the DSI mechanism was part of the original 1992 CBD text

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: India's Legal Framework on ABS (Biological Diversity Act, 2002)
claims.claimText: The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 covers all biological resources within India, making its scope broader than that of the Nagoya Protocol.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: BD Act 2002 covers all biological resources in India, which is broader than the Nagoya Protocol.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question comparing the scope of the BD Act and the Nagoya Protocol.
distractorHooks: The Nagoya Protocol has a broader scope than the BD Act
The BD Act only covers threatened or endemic biological resources

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: India's Legal Framework on ABS (Biological Diversity Act, 2002)
claims.claimText: The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 is implemented through a three-tier statutory system consisting of the National Biodiversity Authority (NBA), State Biodiversity Boards (SBBs), and local Biodiversity Management Committees (BMCs).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Implemented via a three-tier system: NBA (Central), SBBs (State), and BMCs (Local).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the institutional architecture of biodiversity governance in India.
distractorHooks: The BD Act operates on a two-tier system without local committees

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
claims.sectionTitle: India's Legal Framework on ABS (Biological Diversity Act, 2002)
claims.claimText: The National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) possesses the powers of a civil court.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) possesses the powers of a civil court.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question regarding the statutory powers of environmental bodies in India.
distractorHooks: The NBA is purely an advisory body with no judicial powers
The NBA possesses the powers of a criminal court

claims.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations

claims.claimText: Ocean darkening refers to the reduction in light penetration into the oceans, which shrinks the photic zone (the sunlit layer up to 200 meters deep where 90% of marine life thrives).

claims.claimText: Coastal ocean darkening is primarily caused by nutrient, organic material, and sediment loading from agricultural runoff, whereas open ocean darkening is driven by surface warming that leads to algal blooms.

claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question distinguishing the specific drivers of coastal versus open ocean darkening.
distractorHooks: Swapping the causes (e.g., stating sediment loading is the primary cause of open ocean darkening)

claims.claimText: Between 2003 and 2022, 21% of the global ocean became darker, with notable occurrences in the Arctic, Antarctic, and Gulf Stream regions.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: 21% of the global ocean became darker between 2003 and 2022, notably in the Arctic, Antarctic, and Gulf Stream.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the geographical regions most affected by recent ocean darkening trends.
distractorHooks: Claiming ocean darkening is exclusively a tropical or equatorial phenomenon

claims.claimText: The impacts of global ocean darkening include limited ocean productivity, declining fish stocks, and disruptions to climate regulation.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Impacts include limited ocean productivity, declining fish stocks, and affected climate regulation.
claims.examUse: Multi-statement question evaluating the ecological and environmental consequences of ocean darkening.
distractorHooks: Claiming ocean darkening increases overall marine productivity

claims.claimText: Environmental DNA (eDNA) consists of genetic material left by organisms in environments such as water, soil, or air through cells, tissues, fluids, and excrement.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: It is the genetic material, like DNA from cells, tissues, fluids, and excrement, left by organisms in the environment (e.g., water, soil, Air, etc.).

distractorHooks: eDNA can only be extracted directly from living tissue
eDNA is exclusively found in aquatic environments

claims.claimText: eDNA analysis is applied in biodiversity conservation for mapping species presence, determining species distribution, and tracking ecosystems.

claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the practical applications of eDNA technology in conservation.
distractorHooks: eDNA is primarily used for cloning extinct species
eDNA cannot be used to track ecosystem-level changes

claims.claimText: eDNA analysis is a non-invasive, highly sensitive, rapid, and cost-effective biomonitoring method compared to traditional methods.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Significance: Non-invasive, highly sensitive, rapid and cost-effective.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question comparing the advantages of eDNA to traditional biomonitoring methods.
distractorHooks: eDNA is an invasive method requiring physical capture of species

claims.claimText: Blue washing is a deceptive marketing tactic where a company overstates its commitment to responsible, sustainable, social, and ethical business practices.

claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching corporate 'washing' terms with their specific deceptive focus.

claims.claimText: The National Indicator Framework (NIF) Progress Report 2025 was launched by the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: MoSPI launched the National Indicator Framework (NIF) Progress Report 2025.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching national frameworks/reports with the respective Indian ministry.
distractorHooks: Launched by NITI Aayog
Launched by the Ministry of Finance

claims.subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
claims.sectionTitle: Financing for Sustainable Development and the Sevilla Commitment
claims.claimText: The Sevilla Commitment was adopted at the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development to address the $4 trillion annual SDG financing gap.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Sevilla Commitment was adopted at the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development (FFD4) to close the $4 trillion annual SDG financing gap.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the objective and origin of the Sevilla Commitment.
distractorHooks: Associating the commitment with COP28 or UNFCCC instead of FFD4
Stating it addresses only climate finance rather than the broader SDG financing gap

claims.subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
claims.sectionTitle: Financing for Sustainable Development and the Sevilla Commitment
claims.claimText: The United States completely exited the Sevilla Commitment process, which was otherwise adopted by consensus.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Adopted by consensus, lays out a path to close the $4 trillion annual SDG financing gap. United States decided to exit the process entirely.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing knowledge of global participation and consensus in the Sevilla Commitment.
distractorHooks: Claiming the agreement was signed by all UN member states including the US
Stating that developing countries rejected the commitment

claims.subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
claims.sectionTitle: Financing for Sustainable Development and the Sevilla Commitment
claims.claimText: The Debt Swaps for Development Hub is a new financing mechanism led by Spain and the World Bank to scale up debt swaps.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Debt Swaps for Development Hub is led by Spain and the World Bank to scale up debt swaps and lower debt service burdens.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching the initiative with its leading institutions.
distractorHooks: Led by the IMF and the United States
Led by the UN Environment Programme

claims.subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
claims.sectionTitle: Financing for Sustainable Development and the Sevilla Commitment
claims.claimText: SCALED is a blended finance platform that combines public, philanthropic, and private capital for sustainable development.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: SCALED is a blended finance platform combining public, philanthropic, and private capital for sustainable development.
claims.examUse: Definition question identifying the nature of the SCALED platform in SDG financing.
distractorHooks: Defining SCALED as a carbon credit trading mechanism
Describing it as a purely public sovereign wealth fund

claims.subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
claims.sectionTitle: Financing for Sustainable Development and the Sevilla Commitment
claims.claimText: The Coalition for Global Solidarity Levies, led by France, Kenya, and Barbados, aims to tax premium-class flying and private jets to fund climate action.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Coalition for Global Solidarity Levies (led by France, Kenya, Barbados) aims to tax premium-class flying and private jets for climate action.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the specific taxation targets and leadership of the Coalition for Global Solidarity Levies.
distractorHooks: Taxing fossil fuel extraction instead of aviation
Taxing maritime shipping emissions
Led by India and the UK

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: Evolution of Financing for Sustainable Development
claims.claimText: The Monterrey Consensus of 2002 marked the beginning of the United Nations Financing for Development process, focusing on Official Development Assistance.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Monterrey Consensus (2002) marked the beginning of the UN Financing for Development process, focusing on ODA and governance reforms.
claims.examUse: Match-the-following or statement-based question on UN financing milestones.
distractorHooks: Confusing Monterrey Consensus with Doha Declaration
Claiming it established the Green Climate Fund

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: Evolution of Financing for Sustainable Development
claims.claimText: The Doha Declaration of 2008 laid the foundation for the Green Climate Fund.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Doha Declaration (2008) laid the foundation for the Green Climate Fund and added gender and environment understandings to financing.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the origin of the Green Climate Fund.
distractorHooks: Attributing the Green Climate Fund to the Paris Agreement
Attributing the Green Climate Fund to the Addis Ababa Action Agenda

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: Evolution of Financing for Sustainable Development
claims.claimText: The Addis Ababa Action Agenda of 2015 introduced Integrated National Financing Frameworks (INFFs) and the Technology Bank for Least Developed Countries.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Addis Ababa Action Agenda (2015) introduced Integrated National Financing Frameworks (INFFs) and the Technology Bank for Least Developed Countries.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching the agenda to its specific outcomes like INFFs and the Technology Bank.
distractorHooks: Linking INFFs to the Monterrey Consensus
Linking the Technology Bank to the Doha Declaration

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: India's Renewable Energy Sector Status and Targets
claims.claimText: India achieved its NDC target of 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuels five years ahead of the 2030 deadline.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Achieving 50% of cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy resources by 2030 (achieved five years ahead)
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on India's NDC achievements and timelines.
distractorHooks: Claiming the target is yet to be achieved
Stating the target was 40% instead of 50%
Stating the target was achieved exactly in 2030

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: India's Renewable Energy Sector Status and Targets
claims.claimText: Solar power holds the largest share of India's non-fossil fuel installed capacity, followed by Wind and Hydro power.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Solar holds the largest share (39.4%) of India's non-fossil fuel installed capacity, followed by Wind (10.6%) and Hydro (10%).
claims.examUse: Descending order arrangement question of renewable energy sources by installed capacity.
distractorHooks: Claiming Wind or Hydro has the largest share
Swapping the order of Wind and Hydro

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: India's Renewable Energy Sector Status and Targets
claims.claimText: India ranks 4th globally in overall Renewable Energy Installed Capacity and Wind Power capacity, and 3rd in Solar Power capacity.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: India ranks 4th globally in Renewable Energy Installed Capacity and Wind Power, and 3rd in Solar Power capacity.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on India's global rankings in various renewable energy sectors.
distractorHooks: Swapping the rankings (e.g., 3rd in Wind, 4th in Solar)
Claiming India ranks 1st or 2nd globally in RE capacity

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: India's Renewable Energy Sector Status and Targets
claims.claimText: Rajasthan is the top renewable energy state in India, followed by Gujarat and Tamil Nadu.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The top 5 renewable energy states in India are Rajasthan, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Maharashtra.
claims.examUse: Arrangement question for Indian states by renewable energy capacity.
distractorHooks: Placing Gujarat or Tamil Nadu at the top
Including non-top states like Uttar Pradesh or Madhya Pradesh in the top 3

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: India's Renewable Energy Sector Status and Targets
claims.claimText: India's Panchamrita targets for 2030 include reaching 500 GW of non-fossil energy capacity and meeting 50% of energy requirements from renewable energy.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Panchamrita targets for 2030 include meeting 50% of energy requirements from RE and reaching 500 GW of non-fossil energy capacity.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the specific goals outlined in India's Panchamrita strategy.
distractorHooks: Changing the capacity target to 450 GW or 1000 GW
Changing the target year to 2047 or 2050
Stating the target is 100% energy requirements from RE by 2030

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: International Solar Alliance (ISA) and Recent Initiatives
claims.claimText: Mauritius is the first African country to sign the International Solar Alliance's Country Partnership Framework.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Mauritius became the first African country to sign the ISA's Country Partnership Framework (CPF).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on recent international agreements and ISA developments.
distractorHooks: Naming a different African country such as South Africa or Kenya
Claiming Mauritius is the first Asian country

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: International Solar Alliance (ISA) and Recent Initiatives
claims.claimText: The SUNRISE initiative is a global effort focused on the circularity and end-of-life management of solar PV and batteries.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Launch of SUNRISE (Solar Upcycling Network for Recycling, Innovation & Stakeholder Engagement) - a global initiative on circularity and end-of-life (EoL) management of solar PV and batteries
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching environmental initiatives to their specific objectives.
distractorHooks: Claiming SUNRISE focuses on space-based solar power
Claiming SUNRISE is a subsidy scheme for solar panel manufacturing

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: International Solar Alliance (ISA) and Recent Initiatives
claims.claimText: Following a 2020 amendment to its Framework Agreement, all United Nations member states are eligible to join the International Solar Alliance.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Following 2020 amendment to its Framework Agreement, all UN members are now eligible to join.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing knowledge of ISA membership eligibility criteria.
distractorHooks: Claiming membership is strictly limited to countries located between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn
Claiming only developing nations are eligible to join

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: International Solar Alliance (ISA) and Recent Initiatives
claims.claimText: The ISA's 'Towards 1000' strategy aims to mobilize USD 1,000 billion in investments and install 1,000 GW of solar capacity by 2030.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: ISA's 'Towards 1000' strategy aims to mobilize USD 1,000 billion in investments, deliver energy access to 1,000 million people, install 1,000 GW of solar capacity, and mitigate 1,000 million tonnes of CO2 annually by 2030.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on global renewable energy targets and ISA's strategic goals.
distractorHooks: Changing the target year to 2050 or 2070
Altering the target figures, such as 100 GW or USD 100 billion

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: International Solar Alliance (ISA) and Recent Initiatives
claims.claimText: The SolarX Startup Challenge, STAR-C Initiative, Global Solar Facility, and Green Hydrogen Innovation Centre are key initiatives under the International Solar Alliance.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Key ISA initiatives include the SolarX Startup Challenge, STAR-C Initiative, Global Solar Facility, and Green Hydrogen Innovation Centre.
claims.examUse: Multiple-choice question identifying initiatives launched by specific international organizations.
distractorHooks: Attributing these initiatives to the International Energy Agency (IEA)
Attributing these initiatives to the UN Environment Programme (UNEP)

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: Solar Panel Efficiency Norms and ALMM
claims.claimText: The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) has eased solar panel efficiency norms to 18% specifically for off-grid rural projects below 200W.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: MNRE eased solar panel efficiency norms to 18% for both Crystalline silicon and Cadmium Telluride technologies for off-grid rural projects below 200W.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the scope and specific limits of the new solar efficiency norms.
distractorHooks: Claiming the relaxed norms apply to on-grid projects
Claiming the norms apply to projects above 200W
Claiming the efficiency norm was increased rather than eased

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: Solar Panel Efficiency Norms and ALMM
claims.claimText: The Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (ALMM) is a list of solar PV modules and manufacturers approved by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (ALMM) is an MNRE-approved list of solar PV modules and manufacturers.
claims.examUse: Definition or correct-pair question identifying the nodal ministry and purpose of the ALMM.
distractorHooks: Claiming ALMM is maintained by the Ministry of Power or NITI Aayog
Claiming ALMM applies to wind energy components or electric vehicles

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: Solar Panel Efficiency Norms and ALMM
claims.claimText: The relaxed 18% efficiency norms for solar panels do not apply to larger systems such as rooftop solar or solar pumps.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The relaxed efficiency norms apply only to off-grid applications like solar lanterns and micro solar grids, and do not affect larger systems like rooftop solar or pumps.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing the exceptions and exclusions of the relaxed efficiency policy.
distractorHooks: Claiming the relaxed norms apply universally to all solar installations
Claiming rooftop solar and agricultural pumps are included in the relaxed norms

claims.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
claims.sectionTitle: Solar Panel Efficiency Norms and ALMM
claims.claimText: A Distributed Renewable Energy (DRE) category was introduced to support smaller manufacturers and promote wider rural electrification.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: A new Distributed Renewable Energy (DRE) category was introduced to support smaller manufacturers and promote wider rural electrification.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the purpose and target beneficiaries of the newly introduced DRE category.
distractorHooks: Claiming DRE was introduced to support large-scale utility solar parks
Claiming DRE is aimed at foreign manufacturers to boost imports

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.claimText: Photovoltaic cells produce direct current (DC), which must be converted to alternating current (AC) by an inverter for standard use.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Photovoltaic cells produce direct current (DC) which is converted to alternating current (AC) by an inverter.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the basic functioning of solar power technologies.
distractorHooks: Stating that photovoltaic cells directly produce alternating current (AC)
Stating that a transformer is used instead of an inverter to convert DC to AC

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.claimText: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) technology generates energy by focusing sunlight using mirrors, lenses, and tracking devices rather than directly converting light to electricity via photovoltaic effect.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) harnesses energy by focusing sunlight using mirrors, lenses, and tracking devices.
claims.examUse: Comparison question distinguishing between standard photovoltaic (PV) systems and Concentrated Solar Power (CSP).
distractorHooks: Confusing CSP with standard PV cells by stating CSP directly converts sunlight to electricity without focusing it
Stating CSP is only used for agricultural shading

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.claimText: Agriphotovoltaics (APVs), or Solar Farming, is a dual land-use system that simultaneously combines agricultural crop production and solar electricity generation on the same land area.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Agriphotovoltaics (APVs) combine agricultural production and electricity production on the same land area.
claims.examUse: Concept identification or definition-based question on sustainable land-use practices.
distractorHooks: Defining APVs as the use of agricultural biomass to manufacture solar panels
Stating that APVs require completely separate land parcels for farming and electricity generation

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.claimText: Agriphotovoltaic (APV) systems maximize land-use efficiency by utilizing shade-tolerant crops, which are protected from strong solar irradiation and winds by the solar panels.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: APV systems utilize shade-tolerant crops to protect against strong solar irradiation and winds.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the ecological and agricultural synergies of APV systems.
distractorHooks: Stating that APV systems are best suited for crops that require maximum direct sunlight
Claiming that solar panels in APV systems expose crops to higher wind damage

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
claims.claimText: The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) serves as the Nodal Authority for the Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) is the Nodal Authority for the Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the nodal agency for green hydrogen certification.
distractorHooks: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) directly
NITI Aayog
Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
claims.claimText: Green Certification under the GHCI requires an average emission intensity of 2 kg CO2eq/kg H2 or less.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Green Certification is conditional on meeting an average emission intensity requirement of 2 kg CO2eq/kg H2 or less.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing the specific emission threshold for green hydrogen.
distractorHooks: 0 kg CO2eq/kg H2 (absolute zero emissions)
5 kg CO2eq/kg H2
10 kg CO2eq/kg H2

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
claims.claimText: Turquoise Hydrogen is produced through the pyrolysis of methane.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Turquoise Hydrogen is produced through Pyrolysis using Methane
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching hydrogen color codes to their production methods.
distractorHooks: Electrolysis using renewable energy
Coal gasification
Steam methane reforming without carbon capture

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
claims.claimText: Pink Hydrogen is produced using electrolysis powered by nuclear energy.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Pink Hydrogen uses Electrolysis powered by Nuclear Energy.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching hydrogen color codes to their energy sources.
distractorHooks: Electrolysis powered by solar energy
Biomass gasification
Natural gas reforming

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
claims.claimText: The National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) aims to achieve a production target of 5 MMT per annum of Green Hydrogen by 2030.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: NGHM targets the production of 5 MMT per annum of Green Hydrogen by 2030.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the quantitative targets of the NGHM.
distractorHooks: 10 MMT per annum by 2030
5 MMT per annum by 2050
1 MMT per annum by 2025

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
claims.claimText: Deendayal Port, V.O. Chidambaranar Port, and Paradip Port are recognized as Green Hydrogen Hubs under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Deendayal Port, V.O. Chidambaranar Port, and Paradip Port have been recognized as Green Hydrogen Hubs under NGHM.
claims.examUse: Multiple-choice question identifying designated Green Hydrogen Hubs in India.
distractorHooks: Jawaharlal Nehru Port Trust (JNPT)
Cochin Port
Mundra Port

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
claims.claimText: The production of green hydrogen is water-intensive, requiring up to 9 litres of water per kilogram of hydrogen produced.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Green hydrogen production requires up to 9 litres of water per kg of hydrogen and demands high electricity.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the resource requirements and environmental footprint of green hydrogen production.
distractorHooks: Requires no water
Requires 50 litres of water per kg
Uses only atmospheric moisture

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) and Revised Guidelines
claims.claimText: The National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) provides an additional 20% Central Financial Assistance (CFA) for special categories, including the North East Region, hilly states, and SC/ST beneficiaries.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The National Bioenergy Programme provides 20% more Central Financial Assistance (CFA) for special categories like the North East Region, hilly states, and SC/ST beneficiaries.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the financial incentives and target beneficiaries of the National Bioenergy Programme.
distractorHooks: Provides 50% extra CFA
Excludes hilly states from special categories
Only applicable to women entrepreneurs

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) and Revised Guidelines
claims.claimText: The Waste to Energy component of the National Bioenergy Programme supports energy generation through advanced technologies such as pyrolysis and plasma gasification.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Waste to Energy Programme supports projects generating Biogas, BioCNG, Power, or Syngas through technologies like pyrolysis and plasma gasification.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question identifying the specific technologies approved under the Waste to Energy Programme.
distractorHooks: Only supports traditional incineration
Excludes plasma gasification
Limited to solar thermal technologies

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) and Revised Guidelines
claims.claimText: Biogas is primarily composed of 95% methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2), along with trace amounts of other gases like hydrogen sulfide (H2S).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Biogas is composed of 95% methane (CH4) and CO2, with traces of N2, H2, H2S, and O2.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the chemical composition of biogas.
distractorHooks: Primarily composed of nitrogen and oxygen
Contains 95% hydrogen
Does not contain carbon dioxide

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) and Revised Guidelines
claims.claimText: The revised guidelines for the Waste to Energy Programme mandate a two-stage release of Central Financial Assistance, with the first 50% released after obtaining State Pollution Control Board consent.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Revised guidelines for Waste to Energy introduce a two-stage CFA release: 50% after State Pollution Control Board consent and the remainder after 80% capacity achievement.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the implementation and fund disbursal mechanisms of the Waste to Energy Programme.
distractorHooks: 100% upfront release of funds
Requires Central Pollution Control Board consent instead of State
Second installment released immediately after project commissioning

claims.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
claims.sectionTitle: National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) and Revised Guidelines
claims.claimText: Under the Biomass Programme, pellet producers in the National Capital Region (NCR) have the option to choose between schemes from the MNRE or the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) for stubble burning mitigation.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Biomass Programme supports stubble burning mitigation by allowing pellet producers in NCR to choose between MNRE or CPCB schemes.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on institutional frameworks and options available for stubble burning mitigation in the NCR.
distractorHooks: Mandatory to use only the MNRE scheme
Excludes CPCB schemes entirely
Only applicable to farmers in Southern India

claims.sectionTitle: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
claims.claimText: The target for achieving 20% ethanol blending in petrol (E20) was advanced to 2025-26 under the National Policy on Biofuels.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Target of 20% ethanol blending in petrol was advanced to 2025-26 from 2030 under the National Policy on Biofuels.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the targets of the National Policy on Biofuels.
distractorHooks: The target year is 2030
The target is 10% blending by 2025-26

claims.sectionTitle: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
claims.claimText: Ethanol is a high-octane fuel but is 27% less energy-dense than petrol.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Ethanol is 27% less energy-dense than petrol but has a high-octane rating.
claims.examUse: Comparison question on the physical and chemical properties of ethanol versus conventional petrol.
distractorHooks: Ethanol is more energy-dense than petrol
Ethanol has a lower octane rating than petrol

claims.sectionTitle: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
claims.claimText: India is the world's third-largest producer and consumer of ethanol, ranking behind the USA and Brazil.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: India is the world's third-largest producer and consumer of ethanol, after the USA and Brazil.
claims.examUse: Fact-based statement in a multi-statement question regarding India's global standing in biofuel production.
distractorHooks: India is the largest producer of ethanol in the world
India is the second-largest producer of ethanol

claims.sectionTitle: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
claims.claimText: Distillers' dried grains (DDGS) are a by-product of grain ethanol production that are reused as animal feed.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Distillers' dried grains (DDGS) are a by-product of grain ethanol reused as animal feed.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or application-based question on the by-products of biofuel production and their economic utility.
distractorHooks: DDGS is a toxic waste product requiring specialized disposal
DDGS is a by-product of biodiesel production from used cooking oil

claims.sectionTitle: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
claims.claimText: The PM JIVAN Yojana is specifically designed to facilitate and support Second Generation (2G) ethanol projects.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: PM JIVAN Yojana facilitates Second Generation (2G) ethanol projects.
claims.examUse: Scheme-objective matching or statement-based question on government initiatives for advanced biofuels.
distractorHooks: PM JIVAN Yojana supports First Generation (1G) ethanol projects
PM JIVAN Yojana is aimed at promoting solar energy infrastructure

claims.sectionTitle: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
claims.claimText: The GST rate on ethanol meant for the Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme has been reduced to 5%.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: GST on ethanol meant for the EBP Programme was reduced from 18% to 5%.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on government fiscal incentives to promote the Ethanol Blended Petrol Programme.
distractorHooks: Ethanol for the EBP Programme is completely exempted from GST
The GST on ethanol for the EBP Programme is 18%

claims.claimText: 1st Generation biofuels are produced using edible feedstocks such as wheat, corn, sugarcane, and food-grade oils.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: 1st Generation uses edible feedstocks like wheat, corn, sugarcane, and food-grade oils.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the classification of biofuels based on feedstock.
distractorHooks: Stating 1G biofuels use non-edible biomass
Confusing 1G feedstocks with 2G feedstocks like Jatropha

claims.claimText: 2nd Generation biofuels utilize non-edible lignocellulosic biomass and waste materials, including Jatropha, animal fats, and used cooking oil.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: 2nd Generation utilizes lignocellulosic biomass and waste, such as wheat bran, animal fats, used cooking oil, and Jatropha.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching biofuel generations with their respective feedstocks.
distractorHooks: Classifying Jatropha as a 1st Generation feedstock
Stating 2G biofuels are derived from microalgae

claims.claimText: 3rd Generation biofuels are derived from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: 3rd Generation is derived from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question distinguishing 3rd Generation biofuels from other generations.
distractorHooks: Claiming 3rd Generation biofuels use genetically engineered microorganisms
Confusing microalgae with lignocellulosic biomass

claims.claimText: 4th Generation biofuels involve the use of genetically engineered microorganisms such as Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: 4th Generation involves genetically engineered microorganisms like Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the advanced technologies used in 4th Generation biofuels.
distractorHooks: Stating 4th Generation biofuels are derived from naturally occurring algae
Confusing 4G with 3G cyanobacteria biomass

claims.sectionTitle: Alternative Biofuels: Bioethanol, E-Methanol, and Compressed Biogas (CBG)
claims.claimText: Bamboo is classified as a second-generation (2G) ethanol feedstock due to its high lignocellulose content and non-food nature.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Bamboo is an attractive 2G ethanol feedstock due to its high lignocellulose content and non-food nature.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the classification of biofuel generations and their respective feedstocks.
distractorHooks: First-generation (1G) feedstock
Third-generation (3G) feedstock
Low lignocellulose content

claims.sectionTitle: Alternative Biofuels: Bioethanol, E-Methanol, and Compressed Biogas (CBG)
claims.claimText: E-Methanol is a low-carbon fuel produced by combining green hydrogen, derived from water electrolysis, with captured carbon dioxide.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: E-Methanol is a low-carbon fuel produced by combining green hydrogen with captured carbon dioxide.
claims.examUse: Process and composition question regarding alternative low-carbon fuels.
distractorHooks: Grey hydrogen
Blue hydrogen
Combining methane with oxygen

claims.sectionTitle: Alternative Biofuels: Bioethanol, E-Methanol, and Compressed Biogas (CBG)
claims.claimText: The 'Methanol Economy' Programme in India, which promotes the blending of Di-methyl Ether (DME) in LPG, is spearheaded by NITI Aayog.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: NITI Aayog spearheads the 'Methanol Economy' Programme in India, promoting blending of DME (Di-methyl Ether) in LPG.
claims.examUse: Institution-feature question identifying the nodal agency for specific energy transition programs.
distractorHooks: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas
Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

claims.sectionTitle: Alternative Biofuels: Bioethanol, E-Methanol, and Compressed Biogas (CBG)
claims.claimText: Raw biogas is produced through the anaerobic digestion of biomass and primarily consists of 55-60% Methane and 35-40% Carbon dioxide.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Raw biogas is produced by anaerobic digestion of biomass and waste sources. Biogas primarily consists of Methane (55-60%) and Carbon dioxide (35-40%), along with impurities like H2S.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the production process and chemical composition of biogas.
distractorHooks: Aerobic digestion
Primarily consists of Carbon monoxide
Primarily consists of Hydrogen sulfide

claims.claimText: Compressed Biogas (CBG) is produced by purifying raw biogas to remove hydrogen sulfide, carbon dioxide, and water vapor, achieving a methane concentration of 90%.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Raw biogas is purified to remove H2S, CO2, and water vapor, increasing methane concentration to 90%
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the composition and purification process of CBG.
distractorHooks: Methane concentration is 50%
Removes oxygen and nitrogen instead of H2S and CO2

claims.claimText: To produce Compressed Biogas (CBG), purified biogas is compressed to a pressure of 200-250 bar.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: and is then compressed to about 200-250 bar pressure to get CBG.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the physical properties and production of CBG.
distractorHooks: Compressed to 50-100 bar
Compressed to 500 bar

claims.claimText: The GOBAR-DHAN scheme aims to convert cattle dung and farm solid waste into Bio-CNG (CBG) and compost.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The GOBAR-DHAN scheme focuses on converting cattle dung and farm solid waste into Bio-CNG (CBG) and compost.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question matching schemes to their objectives.
distractorHooks: Converts waste to ethanol
Focuses on solar energy
Converts plastic waste

claims.claimText: The SATAT initiative is designed to establish Compressed Biogas (CBG) plants to provide an affordable transportation fuel.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The SATAT initiative aims to set up Compressed Biogas (CBG) plants for affordable transportation.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question matching schemes to their objectives.
distractorHooks: SATAT is for solar panel manufacturing
SATAT is for electric vehicle charging stations

claims.claimText: High-enthalpy geothermal resources (around 200 degrees Celsius) are suitable for electricity generation, whereas low-to-medium enthalpy resources (100-180 degrees Celsius) are suited for direct use.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: High-enthalpy geothermal resources (~200°C) are suitable for electricity generation, while low-to-medium (100-180°C) are for direct use.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the classification and application of geothermal resources based on enthalpy.
distractorHooks: Swapping the applications by stating low-enthalpy resources are used for electricity generation
Claiming that only high-enthalpy resources can be utilized for any geothermal application

claims.claimText: India's estimated geothermal potential is 10,600 MW, with Puga and Chumathang in Ladakh identified as the most promising sites.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: India's estimated geothermal potential is 10,600 MW, with Puga and Chumathang in Ladakh being the most promising sites.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question on India's geothermal potential and key sites.
distractorHooks: Placing Puga and Chumathang in Himachal Pradesh or Uttarakhand
Significantly understating or overstating the 10,600 MW potential

claims.claimText: Among India's 10 Geothermal Provinces, the Himalayan, Naga-Lushai, and Andaman Nicobar provinces are classified as orogenic.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: India has 10 Geothermal Provinces; Himalayan, Naga-Lushai, and Andaman Nicobar are orogenic, while the rest are non-orogenic.
claims.examUse: Classification or statement-based question on the geological nature of Indian geothermal provinces.
distractorHooks: Classifying the Himalayan or Andaman provinces as non-orogenic
Including non-orogenic provinces like Cambay or Son-Narmada-Tapi in the orogenic category

claims.claimText: The National Policy on Geothermal Energy (NPGE) 2025 permits the extraction of geothermal energy from abandoned oil and gas wells and the recovery of mineral by-products like lithium and cesium.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: NPGE 2025 allows extracting geothermal energy from abandoned oil/gas wells and recovering mineral by-products like lithium and cesium under the MMDR Act.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the provisions and scope of NPGE 2025.
distractorHooks: Stating that NPGE 2025 prohibits the use of abandoned oil and gas wells for geothermal extraction
Claiming the policy does not cover the recovery of critical minerals like lithium

claims.claimText: Under the geothermal policy framework, exploration leases can be granted for 3 to 5 years, while development leases can extend up to 30 years.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Exploration leases for geothermal can be granted for 3-5 years, and development leases for up to 30 years.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the regulatory and leasing framework for geothermal energy.
distractorHooks: Swapping the durations of exploration and development leases
Stating that development leases are granted in perpetuity

claims.claimText: Northeast India's first geothermal well is located at Dirang in Arunachal Pradesh and was developed in collaboration with Norway and Iceland.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Northeast's first geothermal well is located at Dirang, Arunachal Pradesh, developed with Norway and Iceland.
claims.examUse: Current affairs or correct-pair question on recent geothermal developments in India.
distractorHooks: Changing the location of the first Northeast geothermal well to Sikkim or Assam
Stating the collaboration was with the USA or France instead of Norway and Iceland

claims.claimText: The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) updated its wind turbine approval system by renaming RLMM to the Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (ALMM) for Wind.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: MNRE updated the wind turbine approval system from RLMM to ALMM (Wind).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on recent renewable energy policy updates and the nodal ministry.
distractorHooks: Ministry of Power instead of MNRE
ALMM applies exclusively to solar energy

claims.claimText: The ALMM (Wind) framework mandates the sourcing of key wind turbine components from approved Indian suppliers and enforces data localization requirements.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: ALMM Wind mandates sourcing key components from approved Indian suppliers and requires data localization.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the specific compliance requirements of the ALMM (Wind) policy.
distractorHooks: Allows 100% imported components without restrictions
Does not require data localization
Mandates technology transfer instead of data localization

claims.claimText: India ranks 4th globally in terms of installed wind power capacity.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: India ranks 4th in the world for installed wind power capacity.
claims.examUse: Fact-based statement in a multi-statement question about India's global standing in renewable energy.
distractorHooks: India ranks 2nd globally
India ranks 5th globally
India ranks 4th in solar power but not wind power

claims.claimText: The National Institute of Wind Energy estimates India's wind power potential to be 1164 GW at 150 meters above ground level.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: India's wind potential is estimated at 1164 GW at 150 meters above ground level by the National Institute of Wind Energy.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on India's renewable energy potential and the assessing agency.
distractorHooks: Potential assessed at 100 meters instead of 150 meters
Assessed by the Central Electricity Authority instead of the National Institute of Wind Energy
Wind potential is estimated at 500 GW

claims.sectionTitle: Draft Guidelines for Underground Coal Gasification (UCG)
claims.claimText: The Draft Guidelines for Underground Coal Gasification (UCG) require companies to maintain an escrow account with the Coal Controller Organization (CCO) to ensure scientific mine closure.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: To ensure scientific mine closure, companies will be required to maintain an escrow account with the Coal Controller Organization (CCO).
claims.examUse: Can be used in a statement-based question regarding the regulatory framework for UCG and the specific responsibilities of the Coal Controller Organization.
distractorHooks: Escrow account is maintained with the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)
Escrow account is maintained with the Reserve Bank of India (RBI)
Escrow account is maintained with the Ministry of Mines

claims.sectionTitle: Coal Gasification and Coal Controller's Organization (CCO)
claims.claimText: The Coal Controller's Organization (CCO) acts as the appellate authority for disputes between consumers and owners regarding the declaration of coal grade and size.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: CCO acts as the appellate authority for disputes between consumers and owners regarding the declaration of coal grade and size.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the statutory functions and dispute resolution mechanisms of the CCO.
distractorHooks: Attributing this appellate role to Coal India Limited (CIL)
Attributing this role to the Ministry of Coal directly

claims.sectionTitle: Coal Gasification and Coal Controller's Organization (CCO)
claims.claimText: Coal gasification produces syngas, which is a mixture consisting of carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen (H2), carbon dioxide (CO2), and methane (CH4).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Syngas is a mixture of carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen (H2), carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and other gases.
claims.examUse: Statement-based or multiple-choice question identifying the constituent gases of syngas.
distractorHooks: Including sulfur dioxide (SO2) or nitrogen oxides (NOx) as primary components
Stating syngas is exclusively methane

claims.sectionTitle: Coal Gasification and Coal Controller's Organization (CCO)
claims.claimText: The Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS) aims to achieve 100 million tonnes of coal gasification by the year 2030.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS) aims to achieve 100 million tonnes of coal gasification by 2030.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing factual knowledge of government energy transition targets.
distractorHooks: Changing the target year to 2050 or 2070
Changing the target volume to 50 or 500 million tonnes

claims.claimText: The ADEETIE scheme is implemented by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) under the Ministry of Power.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: ADEETIE is implemented by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) under the Ministry of Power.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the correct implementing agency and nodal ministry.
distractorHooks: Ministry of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSME)
Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
Small Industries Development Bank of India (SIDBI)

claims.claimText: The ADEETIE scheme targets MSMEs possessing a Udyam ID to help them reduce energy consumption by 30-50%.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: It targets MSMEs with a Udyam ID to reduce energy consumption by 30-50%.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing the eligibility criteria and the specific energy reduction target.
distractorHooks: Large scale industries
MSMEs without Udyam ID
Energy reduction target of 10-20%

claims.claimText: Under the ADEETIE scheme, Micro and Small Enterprises receive a 5% interest subvention, while Medium Enterprises receive a 3% interest subvention.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The scheme provides a 5% interest subvention for Micro and Small Enterprises and 3% for Medium Enterprises.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question matching the enterprise category with its respective interest subvention rate.
distractorHooks: Flat 5% interest subvention for all MSMEs
3% for Micro and Small Enterprises and 5% for Medium Enterprises
Capital subsidy instead of interest subvention

claims.claimText: The ADEETIE scheme has a budgetary outlay of ₹1000 crore and is designed for a 3-year duration from FY 2025-26 to FY 2027-28.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The budgetary outlay is ₹1000 crore over a 3-year duration (FY 2025-26 to FY 2027-28).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question verifying the financial outlay and implementation timeline of the scheme.
distractorHooks: 5-year duration
₹5000 crore outlay

claims.sectionTitle: CAFE Norms and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)
claims.claimText: CAFE-3 norms will be effective from April 1, 2027, to March 31, 2032.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: CAFE-3 norms will come into force from 1st April 2027 until 31st March 2032.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the implementation timeline of CAFE-3 norms.
distractorHooks: Implementation starting in 2024 or 2025
Applicable until 2035

claims.sectionTitle: CAFE Norms and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)
claims.claimText: CAFE norms apply to passenger cars with a seating capacity not exceeding 9 persons and a gross vehicle weight not exceeding 3,500 kg.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: CAFE norms apply to passenger cars with a seating capacity not exceeding 9 persons and a gross vehicle weight not exceeding 3,500 kg.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the applicability criteria of CAFE norms.
distractorHooks: Applies to commercial heavy vehicles
Weight limit of 5,000 kg
Seating capacity up to 12 persons

claims.sectionTitle: CAFE Norms and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)
claims.claimText: The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) was established under the legislative framework of the Energy Conservation Act 2001.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: BEE was established under the legislative framework of the Energy Conservation Act 2001.
claims.examUse: Institution-feature question regarding the statutory backing of BEE.
distractorHooks: Established under the Environment Protection Act 1986

claims.sectionTitle: CAFE Norms and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)
claims.claimText: The State Energy Efficiency Index (SEEI) 2024 was developed by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) in collaboration with the Alliance for an Energy Efficient Economy.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The State Energy Efficiency Index (SEEI) 2024 was developed by BEE in collaboration with the Alliance for an Energy Efficient Economy.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question on the publishers of major indices.
distractorHooks: Published by NITI Aayog
Published by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)

claims.sectionTitle: Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRCs) and National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF)
claims.claimText: Under the National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF), Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRCs) receive Rs 1 lakh in financial assistance to set up cluster-level enterprises for supplying biological inputs.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: BRCs provide Rs 1 lakh financial assistance for setting up cluster-level enterprises to supply biological inputs.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the financial assistance and purpose of Bio-Input Resource Centres.
distractorHooks: Rs 5 lakh financial assistance
District-level enterprises
Supply of chemical fertilizers

claims.sectionTitle: Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRCs) and National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF)
claims.claimText: The National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme with a funding pattern of 90:10 for Hilly and North Eastern States and 60:40 for other States.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: NMNF is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme with a 90:10 funding ratio for Hilly & North Eastern States and 60:40 for all other States.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the scheme type and funding pattern of NMNF.
distractorHooks: Central Sector Scheme (100% funded by the Centre)
50:50 funding ratio for all other States
100% funding for Hilly and North Eastern States

claims.sectionTitle: Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRCs) and National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF)
claims.claimText: The National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF) targets reaching 1 crore farmers and initiating natural farming across a 7.5 lakh hectare area by 2025-26.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: NMNF aims to reach 1 crore farmers and initiate Natural Farming in a 7.5 lakh Ha area by 2025-26.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the specific targets and timeline of the NMNF.
distractorHooks: Target year of 2030
5 crore farmers
15 lakh hectare area

claims.sectionTitle: Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRCs) and National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF)
claims.claimText: Under the National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF), 30,000 Krishi Sakhis (Community Resource Persons) are utilized to scale up natural farming practices.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: 30,000 Krishi Sakhis (Community Resource Persons) are utilized for the easy scaling up of Natural Farming practices.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the role of Krishi Sakhis in agricultural schemes.
distractorHooks: Krishi Sakhis are utilized exclusively for digital literacy among farmers

claims.sectionTitle: Natural Farming Practices and Comparison with Organic Farming
claims.claimText: Beejamrit is a seed treatment component of Natural Farming that utilizes cow dung, urine, and lime-based formulations.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Beejamrit involves the treatment of seeds using cow dung, urine, and lime-based formulations.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair questions matching traditional farming terms with their specific agricultural purpose.
distractorHooks: Confusing Beejamrit with a bio-stimulant like Jivamrit
Defining Beejamrit as a chemical pesticide or fertilizer

claims.sectionTitle: Natural Farming Practices and Comparison with Organic Farming
claims.claimText: Whapasa is a Natural Farming practice that refers to activating earthworms in the soil to facilitate water vapour condensation.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Whapasa refers to activating earthworms in the soil for water vapour condensation.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair questions matching farming terminology with its ecological function.
distractorHooks: Describing Whapasa as a method of deep plowing
Confusing Whapasa with seed treatment or mulching

claims.sectionTitle: Natural Farming Practices and Comparison with Organic Farming
claims.claimText: Unlike Organic Farming, which allows off-farm purchased inputs, Natural Farming relies strictly on on-farm inputs based on the Desi Cow.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Organic Farming involves off-farm purchased inputs, whereas Natural Farming relies strictly on on-farm inputs based on the Desi Cow.
claims.examUse: Statement-based questions comparing the input requirements of Natural Farming versus Organic Farming.
distractorHooks: Stating that Natural Farming requires expensive off-farm organic inputs
Claiming Organic Farming strictly prohibits any off-farm inputs

claims.sectionTitle: Natural Farming Practices and Comparison with Organic Farming
claims.claimText: Natural Farming does not permit the use of compost, vermicompost, or mined minerals for soil correction, whereas Organic Farming does.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Natural Farming does not allow the use of compost, vermicompost, or mined minerals for soil correction, unlike Organic Farming.
claims.examUse: Statement-based questions testing the specific restrictions of Natural Farming.
distractorHooks: Asserting that vermicompost is a core component of Natural Farming
Stating that both Natural and Organic farming rely heavily on mined minerals for soil correction

claims.sectionTitle: Natural Farming Practices and Comparison with Organic Farming
claims.claimText: Natural Farming relies on surface decomposition rather than plowing.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Unlike Organic Farming, Natural Farming uses no external inputs, avoids compost/minerals for soil correction, and relies on surface decomposition rather than plowing.
claims.examUse: Statement-based questions identifying the core soil management practices of Natural Farming.
distractorHooks: Claiming that deep plowing is essential for Natural Farming to aerate the soil
Stating that Natural Farming requires mechanical tilling for weed control

claims.claimText: The Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS) Programme is operated by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: GIAHS is a programme operated by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).
claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the nodal international organization responsible for the GIAHS programme.
distractorHooks: UNESCO
UNEP
World Economic Forum

claims.claimText: The traditional agroforestry system for the erva-mate plant in Brazil is recognized as a Globally Important Agricultural Heritage System (GIAHS).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Six new sites were added to the FAO's GIAHS Programme, including a traditional agroforestry system for the erva-mate plant in Brazil
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching traditional agricultural systems to their respective countries.
distractorHooks: Peru
Argentina
Mexico

claims.claimText: Specialised farming systems for pearl mussels, white tea, and pears in China are recognized as Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS).
claims.evidenceExcerpt: specialised farming systems in China (pearl mussels, white tea, pears)
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching agricultural heritage systems to their respective countries.
distractorHooks: Japan
South Korea
Vietnam

claims.claimText: The Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS) programme was launched at the World Summit for Sustainable Development in 2002.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Launched at the World Summit for Sustainable Development in 2002, the programme protects family farming and traditional systems.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on the origin and timeline of the GIAHS programme.
distractorHooks: Launched at the 1992 Earth Summit in Rio
Launched during the Paris Climate Agreement in 2015

claims.claimText: GIAHS protects traditional agricultural systems, family farming, and agrobiodiversity from threats such as climate change, community displacements, and biodiversity loss.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Protects traditional agricultural systems from climate change, community displacements, and biodiversity loss.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question identifying the core objectives and focus areas of GIAHS.
distractorHooks: Focuses primarily on promoting modern, high-yield commercial agriculture

claims.claimText: India has three recognized GIAHS sites: Saffron Heritage of Kashmir, Koraput Traditional Agriculture in Odisha, and Kuttanad Below Sea Level Farming System in Kerala.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: India has three GIAHS sites: Saffron Heritage of Kashmir, Koraput Traditional Agriculture (Odisha), and Kuttanad Below Sea Level Farming System (Kerala).
claims.examUse: Correct-pair question matching Indian GIAHS sites to their respective states or identifying recognized sites.
distractorHooks: Mismatching states like Koraput in Chhattisgarh or Kuttanad in Tamil Nadu
Including non-GIAHS sites like Darjeeling Tea estates or Kangra Valley agriculture

claims.claimText: Under Model Rules 2025, felling trees on agricultural land requires a Felling Permit for more than 10 trees, and a No Objection Certificate (NOC) for 10 or fewer trees.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Model Rules 2025 require Felling Permits for agricultural lands with more than 10 trees, and NOCs for 10 or fewer trees.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question on environmental regulations and agricultural land management.
distractorHooks: Swapping the threshold to 50 trees
Stating that all tree felling requires a Felling Permit regardless of number

claims.claimText: The felling of trees on agricultural land under Model Rules 2025 is overseen by State Level Committees, requiring applicants to register in the National Timber Management System.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: The Government issued Model Rules 2025 for felling trees on agricultural land, overseen by State Level Committees. Applicants register in the National Timber Management System.
claims.examUse: Institution-feature question identifying the regulatory bodies and portals involved in timber management.
distractorHooks: Oversight by Central Level Committees or the National Green Tribunal
